title: Porosity and Indications from Specific Material Forms
narrationScript: Porosity and cracks bleed out differently, and a Level Two inspector reads that difference. A crack emerges as a continuous line from the first minute of development and only widens. A surface pore blooms as a round dot, and a row of pores can merge into something that mimics a crack. When in doubt, wipe the indication, redevelop, and watch how it re-forms. Most acceptance criteria call an indication linear when its length is at least three times its width, so classification decides acceptance. Every product form leaves a signature. Forgings show wavy, faint laps, straight tight seams, and ragged bursts on machined faces. Castings show smooth cold shuts, lacy microshrinkage patches, and scattered gas pores. Plate hides laminations until a cut edge exposes them. Welds show porosity patterns and straight fusion-line indications at the toe. Extrusions show transverse hot-short tears on the surface, with central bursts and back-end pipe at cut ends. Penetrant reads nonmetals too: glass cracks crisply, glaze crazes in spiderwebs, and plastics demand a compatibility check first.

sections.heading: Porosity: The Rounded Indication Family
sections.body:
Porosity is gas that came out of solution or was trapped as the metal froze — or the void left behind where feed metal ran out. When a pore breaks the surface, penetrant charges the cavity and developer pulls it back out as a **round or nearly round indication**. Read three things from it.

- **Shape over time.** A pore blooms radially — a dot that grows into a wider dot. A crack draws a line from its very first bleedout. The shape at first appearance carries more information than the blob you find an hour later, so watch suspect areas as development starts.
- **Bleed volume.** A pinhole mouth over a large subsurface cavity bleeds profusely and **regenerates after wiping** — strong evidence of real volume below. A shallow surface pit gives one weak bloom and quits.
- **Distribution.** Isolated, scattered pores point to general gas evolution. A tight **cluster** points to a local cause — a damp core, a contaminated patch of mold. A **row of aligned pores** deserves your full attention, because as bleedout spreads, the dots can merge into something that looks linear.

Porosity is rarely random. Note the pattern, then ask what the process was doing at that exact spot.

sections.caption: Cracks bleed as continuous lines; pores bloom as discrete dots — and a row of pores can merge into a false linear. The bleedback check separates them.

sections.heading: Rounded or Linear? Classification and the Bleedback Check
sections.body:
Acceptance criteria treat the two families differently, and the split is almost always defined the same way: **a linear indication has a length at least three times its width; anything else is rounded**. Your governing specification's definitions control, but that 3-to-1 convention is what you will meet in most aerospace and weld acceptance criteria — and linear indications nearly always carry the tighter limits, because cracks are linear.

Two disciplines make the call reliable:

- **Read inside the window.** Bleedout never stops spreading. Development commonly starts at a 10-minute minimum, and an indication evaluated hours late has grown into a wide, soft blob that hides its true shape, merges with its neighbors, and inflates every measurement. You measure the indication, so *when* you measure matters.
- **The bleedback check.** Where the written procedure permits, wipe the indication once with a solvent-dampened swab, apply a thin fresh coat of developer, and watch it re-form. A crack re-bleeds as a **continuous line**. Aligned porosity re-bleeds as **separate beads** that grow individually before they touch.

Many criteria also group closely spaced rounded indications and evaluate the row as a unit — so document the spacing, not just the dots.

sections.heading: Forgings: Laps, Bursts, and Seams
sections.body:
Forging works hot metal under dies, and its discontinuities follow the metal flow.

- **Laps** form when a fin or corner of metal folds over and is pressed into the surface without fusing — the oxide film prevents the weld. Look for them at **flash lines, fillets, and re-entrant corners**. A lap meets the surface at a shallow angle and its opening is tight and oxide-lined, so the indication is **wavy, irregular, faint, and often intermittent**. A tight lap may need extended penetrant dwell to charge at all. Burn this one in: faint and wavy does not mean ignorable — it is the classic lap signature.
- **Bursts** are ruptures from forging at the wrong temperature or taking too much reduction at once. They usually live inside the part, so PT meets them **on machined surfaces that cut into the burst** — a ragged, jagged linear indication on a freshly cut face.
- **Seams** ride in on the bar stock — ingot-era cracks and blowholes elongated by rolling. They run **straight and parallel to the grain direction**, continuous or intermittent, and tight enough to bleed weakly.

When a forging shows a linear indication, orientation and texture tell you which of the three you are looking at.

sections.heading: Castings: Cold Shuts, Shrinkage, and Gas
sections.body:
Castings freeze from liquid, and their indications tell solidification stories.

- **Cold shuts** form where two streams of metal met too cold to fuse — an interrupted pour or a long, thin flow path. The indication is a **smooth, narrow line, continuous or broken, with rounded contours**, often curving gently across the surface. Smoothness is the discriminator: a hot tear (last lesson) is ragged; a cold shut is sleek.
- **Shrinkage** comes in two scales. **Macroshrinkage** — cavities where feed metal ran out — concentrates at section changes and under risers; PT sees it where it reaches the surface or where machining opens it, as irregular, ragged-walled voids that bleed heavily. **Microshrinkage** is interdendritic and fine: a **lacy, mottled patch** of indication rather than a discrete line or dot, common in magnesium and aluminum castings.
- **Gas porosity** shows as rounded dots, scattered or clustered. Subsurface gas holes opened by machining appear as clean round cavities that bleed and regenerate.

As-cast surfaces are rough and hold background. Expect cleaner reads on machined areas, and judge patchy background against your process — not every glow is a flaw.

sections.caption: Each product form leaves a signature: where the process put the discontinuity, and an orientation that follows the metal flow.

sections.heading: Plate and Extrusions: Edges and Ends Tell the Story
sections.body:
Rolling and extrusion stretch every discontinuity along the working direction — so the inspection logic is directional.

- **Plate laminations** are ingot pipe, porosity, and inclusion clusters flattened into planar separations **parallel to the plate faces**. They do not break the rolled surface, so the faces show nothing. PT finds laminations **only on cut, sheared, or machined edges**, as straight linear indications at or near mid-thickness. When a fabrication procedure penetrant-tests plate edges before welding, this is why.
- **Stringers** — elongated nonmetallic inclusions — show on edges the same way: thin, straight, intermittent lines in the rolling direction.
- **Extrusions** carry their billet's history down the centerline. Billet pipe and oxidized billet skin flow toward the **back end** of the extrusion as an internal defect, and **central bursts** — chevron-shaped internal ruptures from too-severe reduction — also hide inside. Both surface only where a saw cut or machining exposes them, so inspect **cut ends**. On the outside, running too hot or too fast tears the skin in **short transverse cracks** — hot shortness, or speed cracking — the one extrusion discontinuity that runs across the flow. Shallow longitudinal die lines, by contrast, bleed weakly and uniformly.

The Level II habit: ask which way the metal flowed, then check the surfaces that cut across it.

sections.heading: Welds: Porosity Patterns and Fusion-Line Indications
sections.body:
A weld is a casting made in seconds, so welds show porosity and fusion discontinuities with their own grammar.

- **Scattered porosity** across a bead suggests general gas trouble — damp electrodes, lost shielding. A **cluster** marks a local event: an arc restart, a spot of contamination. **Aligned porosity**, a row of rounded indications tracking the centerline or a fusion line, points to contamination along the joint preparation, and many acceptance criteria evaluate the aligned row as a group.
- **Piping porosity** is an elongated gas channel. Where it reaches the surface, PT shows a single round indication that **bleeds heavily and regenerates after wiping** — small mouth, big cavity.
- **Crater discontinuities** sit at weld stops: shrinkage pits that bleed as rounded spots, and the star-shaped crater cracks you met with the crack families.
- **Lack of fusion and overlap** break the surface along the **toe or fusion line** as a straight, tight linear indication tracing the boundary between weld and base metal. Overlap — weld metal rolled over the plate without fusing — leaves a penetrant-holding crevice at the toe.

As-welded surfaces fight you: ripples, spatter, and undercut grooves all trap penetrant. Sorting those indications — true, false, relevant, nonrelevant — is the next lesson.

sections.caption: Metallic and nonmetallic materials compared — penetrant practices such as ASTM E1417 cover nonporous metals and nonmetals alike.
rows: Wrought metals (steel, aluminum, titanium, nickel alloys)
Laps, seams, laminations at edges, bursts on machined faces, processing and service cracks
Mostly linear; tight openings bleed weakly and read faint
Smeared metal from machining, grinding, or blasting can seal openings — etch when the governing specification requires it
Cast metals
Cold shuts, surface shrinkage, gas porosity, hot tears
Smooth lines, lacy patches, rounded dots
As-cast roughness raises background; machined areas read cleaner than as-cast surfaces
Glass
Thermal-shock and impact cracks, deep scratches
Crisp, bright, continuous lines
Smooth nonporous surface gives near-ideal capillary response — even faint lines are meaningful
Glazed ceramics
Crazing of the glaze, thermal-shock cracks, chips
Fine spiderweb networks; sharp single lines
Unglazed or porous ceramic floods with background and is generally impractical to inspect
Rigid plastics
Molding and stress cracks, impact damage
Sharp lines, much like metal cracks
Verify penetrant, remover, and developer compatibility first — some polymers craze, swell, or dissolve

figures.title: Typical PT indications by product form

interactive.title: Trace the Indication to Its Process
interactive.intro: A Level II reads an indication and asks what process could have left it there. Sort each penetrant indication into the product form that most likely produced it.

items.label: Smooth, narrow line with rounded contours, curving gently across the surface
items.why: Two metal streams met too cold to fuse — the cold shut's smooth, sleek line is a casting signature. Ragged edges would say hot tear instead.

items.label: Lacy, mottled patch of fine indications in a thick section near a boss
items.why: Interdendritic microshrinkage bleeds as a lacy patch where the last metal froze without feed — common in magnesium and aluminum castings.

items.label: Faint, wavy, intermittent line at a fillet near the flash line
items.why: A forging lap: metal folded over and pressed in without fusing. Tight, oxide-lined, and at a shallow angle to the surface — hence faint and wavy.

items.label: Straight line at mid-thickness on a sheared plate edge, parallel to the faces
items.why: A lamination — flattened ingot pipe, porosity, or inclusions. It lies parallel to the rolled faces, so only an edge that cuts across it can show it.

items.label: Chevron-shaped indications centered on the sawed end of a bar
items.why: Central bursts from too-severe extrusion reduction live on the centerline and surface only where a cut end or machining exposes them.

items.label: Row of rounded dots tracking the centerline of a bead
items.why: Aligned porosity — gas trailing the molten puddle, usually from contamination along the joint preparation. Many criteria evaluate the row as a group.

items.label: Single round spot that bleeds heavily and regenerates after wiping
items.why: Piping porosity: an elongated gas channel reaching the surface through a small mouth. Big subsurface volume keeps feeding the bleedout.

items.label: Straight, tight line hugging the toe where bead meets base metal
items.why: Lack of fusion or overlap at the toe traces the fusion boundary as a straight linear indication — a crevice between weld and base metal.

checkQuestions.question: During development, an indication along a weld centerline first appears as separate rounded dots that slowly merge into a single line. After a bleedback check (wipe with a solvent-dampened swab, thin fresh developer), separate beads re-form and grow individually. What is the indication?
options: A centerline crack
Aligned porosity from contamination along the joint
A cold shut
Lack of fusion at the weld toe
checkQuestions.explanation: A crack re-bleeds as a continuous line from the first moment. Discrete beads that regenerate individually are the signature of a row of gas pores — aligned porosity — which only looked linear because spreading bleedout merged the dots.

checkQuestions.question: Why are forging laps notorious for producing faint, intermittent indications?
options: They are always fully subsurface, so no penetrant can enter
They occur only on as-forged surfaces too rough to inspect
Developer will not adhere over folded metal
The fold is tight, oxide-lined, and meets the surface at a shallow angle, so it traps very little penetrant
checkQuestions.explanation: A lap is folded-over metal pressed in without fusion. Its opening is tight, contaminated with oxide, and oblique to the surface, so it holds a minimal volume of penetrant — producing weak, wavy, intermittent indications. Extended dwell helps charge them.

checkQuestions.question: Rolled plate contains laminations. Where can PT reveal them?
options: On cut, sheared, or machined edges only, because laminations lie parallel to the plate faces
Anywhere on the rolled faces, since rolling forces laminations up to the surface
Only after etching the rolled faces to open the laminations
Nowhere — laminations are volumetric and never reach any surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Laminations are planar separations flattened parallel to the plate surfaces, so the rolled faces show nothing. An edge that cuts across the plane exposes the separation, which then bleeds as a straight linear indication near mid-thickness.

checkQuestions.question: An aluminum casting shows a smooth, narrow, gently curving linear indication with rounded contours. What is the most likely source?
options: A hot tear from restrained solidification
Microshrinkage
A cold shut where two streams of metal met without fusing
A grinding crack
checkQuestions.explanation: Smooth, sleek contours are the cold-shut discriminator. Hot tears are ragged and jagged, microshrinkage reads as a lacy mottled patch rather than a single line, and grinding cracks form sharp networks on ground surfaces.

checkQuestions.question: Before penetrant testing a rigid plastic component, what must be verified first?
options: That the part is ferromagnetic
That the penetrant, remover, and developer are compatible with the material and will not craze or attack it
That the part has been etched like a machined metal part
That the part is electrically conductive
checkQuestions.explanation: PT applies to nonporous nonmetals, but some penetrant oils and solvents craze, swell, or dissolve polymers, so material compatibility comes first. Magnetism and conductivity are irrelevant to PT, and etching is a remedy for smeared metal, not plastics.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Level II Topical Outline §3.3–3.5 (indications from porosity and specific material forms; metallic and nonmetallic materials)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (scope, processing, and evaluation requirements, general reference)
ASTM E433, standard reference photographs for liquid penetrant inspection (indication appearance reference)
ASME BPVC Section VIII, Mandatory Appendix 8 (linear vs rounded indication definitions, acceptance criteria context)
